title: An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say! (2)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, legal chronology, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Legal allegations and findings remain attributed to the parties and courts described in the letter. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy. Page images 107-115 were used to remove a duplicated title fragment and page-header debris, restore dropped text, repair company names and mechanical OCR joins, and recover the Public Works Department valuation sentence. The closing page visibly prints `21-10-2013`; that source-printed date is preserved despite its apparent chronological tension with the surrounding 2014 proceedings. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: This series, begun yesterday and continued today, is not meant to wound anyone. It is published with a constructive purpose: that both ruling and opposition parties may use it to understand democratic norms; that it may stand as an obstacle to authoritarian practices knowingly pursued without restraint; and that both sides may examine themselves and set right the path before them. It is not published out of hatred, hostility or a desire for vengeance. Those who come to power must uphold dignity and duty in politics, guide truthfully the people who voted for them, give no room to arrogance, and stand as examples of affection and righteousness. If this letter helps even a little toward that end, I shall have the satisfaction of having rendered a good service to the country as I continue.

In yesterday's letter, I explained that the legality under the Constitution of establishing a Special Court and appointing a Special Judge in Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case was upheld all the way to the Supreme Court.
In that Special Court, evidence was recorded from 28 December 1998 to 24 August 2000. The prosecution examined 258 witnesses and submitted documentary and material evidence.

Tamil Nadu then underwent another change of government in May 2001, and Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister. Proceedings against Jayalalithaa and the other three resumed in Chennai in November 2002 before Judge R. Rajamanickam under the AIADMK Government. Seventy-six witnesses who had already testified and been cross-examined were recalled for further cross-examination. Of them, sixty-four retracted their earlier testimony. The Public Prosecutor, who ought under law to have declared them hostile witnesses, did not do so because he had been appointed by the AIADMK Government.

Under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, accused persons must appear in court and answer questions. Yet under the AIADMK Government, the accused were not brought to court. Instead, the extraordinary practice of going to their homes, questioning them there and recording their statements was permitted. The Public Prosecutor, who should have objected to this complete departure from procedure, raised no objection.

It was then that our DMK General Secretary, Professor Anbazhagan, petitioned the Supreme Court to transfer the trial to another State. Because the Supreme Court was said to have stayed the proceedings, the trial stopped for a short time. In its judgment of 18 November 2003 on the Professor's petition, however, the Supreme Court ordered the case transferred to Karnataka.
In that order, Justices S.N. Variava and H.K. Sema said in substance:

Counsel appearing for Jayalalithaa argued that the petitioner, the Professor, was a political enemy and had approached the Supreme Court for political reasons. That argument could not be accepted. In a democracy, opposition parties occupy an important place both inside and outside the legislature. They bear the vital responsibility of watching those in power, and their principal weapon is to oppose the ruling party's wrongful practices and actions. They generally give voice to public grievances. As a member of the opposition, the petitioner therefore had a genuine interest in the State's administration and administration of justice. His petition could not be dismissed as merely political.

The petitioner had raised several reasonable and acceptable grounds creating a strong apprehension that justice might be denied or administered one-sidedly. The Court agreed that these concerns were well founded.

The extent to which the prosecution had acted in concert with the accused was evident. After Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister, witnesses were recalled on some ordinary pretext, and the Public Prosecutor appointed by her Government allowed it without objection. That fact alone showed how far justice had been diverted in the case. The new Public Prosecutor appeared to be acting hand in glove with the accused, creating in the public mind a reasonable apprehension that justice would fail. The process of justice was clearly being subverted.

It was regrettable that the Public Prosecutor stood by without objecting to Jayalalithaa's improper application to avoid appearing personally for questioning under Section 313. However high one might be, the law stood higher. In short, this was an attempt to obstruct the course of justice. The petitioner had raised reasonable and meaningful apprehensions of diversion and bias requiring the Court's intervention.

Those were the observations made by the two Supreme Court Judges not today but in 2003, eleven years ago, when they transferred the case from Tamil Nadu to Bengaluru. I have repeated their words so that those who now accuse us of filing some false case may kindly reflect upon them.

Acting on the Supreme Court's directions, the Karnataka Government authorised a Special Court by notification dated 19 February 2005 and appointed senior advocate B.V. Acharya as Special Public Prosecutor.

The record of the subsequent proceedings shows that the accused filed petition after petition in the Special Court on various grounds. Since they had the right to do so and the trial had to remain fair and just, these applications were permitted. Judge Cunha observed in his judgment that the trial was greatly delayed because the accused challenged every order of the Bengaluru Special Court first in the Karnataka High Court and then in the Supreme Court.

The Special Public Prosecutor recalled forty-five witnesses for cross-examination. On 14 July 2010, Jayalalithaa petitioned the Special Court to cancel all English translations of the witness statements, prepare fresh and accurate translations, recall every witness and record their statements afresh.
The Special Court rejected this petition on 22 July 2010. Jayalalithaa appealed to the Karnataka High Court and then to the Supreme Court.

Jayalalithaa's examination under Section 313 of the Code of Criminal Procedure began on 20 October 2011. After answering 1,337 questions, she submitted a written statement on 23 November 2011. While Sasikala's examination was halfway through, she petitioned the Special Court on 29 February 2012 seeking documents that had neither been cited nor filed. Her petition was rejected on 3 April 2012. She approached the Karnataka High Court, which also dismissed it, and then approached the Supreme Court. On 27 September 2012, the Supreme Court directed that Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi be allowed to inspect the specified documents within twenty-one days.

Sasikala then answered the remaining questions put to her. Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi were also examined, and Sudhakaran filed a written statement on 21 January 2013. Counsel for Jayalalithaa and Sasikala argued from 5 to 14 August 2013. During this period, the Professor applied for permission to assist the Public Prosecutor. His application was accepted; he was allowed to submit written arguments and directed to provide whatever assistance the Special Public Prosecutor required.

The Special Public Prosecutor began his arguments on 23 August 2013. While he was arguing, the Karnataka Government issued an order withdrawing Bhavani Singh as Special Public Prosecutor. Jayalalithaa's side petitioned the Supreme Court insisting that Bhavani Singh himself should continue. The Supreme Court sent the matter to the Karnataka High Court to examine whether Karnataka's order would help bring the case to an early conclusion.
Meanwhile, the term of the Special Judge, who was due to retire at the end of September 2013, was extended. When he nevertheless retired on 31 October 2013, Judge Cunha was appointed Special Judge.
After Judge Cunha took office, steps were taken to bring the material objects and valuable properties held by the Special Court in Chennai to Bengaluru. Arguments resumed on 7 March 2014.

At this stage, five companies, Lex Property Development Limited, Meadow Agro Farms Private Limited, Riverway Agro Products Private Limited, Signora Business Enterprises Private Limited and Ramraj Agro Mills Limited, filed separate petitions. They sought disposal of their pending cases asking the Chief Judge of the Chennai Court of Small Causes to lift orders freezing their properties. These petitions were deliberately intended to delay the principal case. The Special Court ordered that arguments in them be heard simultaneously with the main case. Arguments thus began for the third time.
Special Judge Cunha stated in his judgment:

“I heard the detailed submissions of the Special Public Prosecutor. After reading the examination of the prosecution witnesses, he consolidated and presented his arguments. P. Kumar, counsel for the accused, addressed every aspect of the case in detail over eighty hours. He strongly attacked the manner in which the Investigating Officer had conducted the investigation and alleged many errors.

“In his brief reply, the Special Public Prosecutor submitted that the case had been instituted according to the required procedures and action had been taken under the directions of the Madras High Court; that there was no illegality; that all documents had been filed before the Court; and that the accused could raise no objection at this stage. He further submitted that the valuations made by Public Works Department engineers were correct, and that most documents filed by the accused had been created only after charges were framed on 25 June 1997.”
Although Judge Cunha continued to record these matters throughout his judgment, he stated his conclusion concisely only at the end. I wish first to present that summary.

“During Jayalalithaa's tenure as Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu from 1991 to 1996, her income was Rs. 9,91,05,094. But the accused possessed no proper records showing how that income had arisen, and they could not provide reasonable accounts for it. At the stage of judgment, the accused placed certain matters before the Court for consideration before sentence was imposed.

“The first accused, Jayalalithaa, stated that the case had been brought by her political opponents for political reasons and to take revenge upon her; that she was forty-eight when it began; that it had dragged on for eighteen years; that she was now sixty-six; and that during this period the case had caused unending mental distress and seriously damaged her health, leaving her with diabetes, high blood pressure, breathing difficulty and other ailments. She asked the Court to consider these circumstances when delivering judgment.”
After summarising what the accused had said, the Judge gave his ruling:
“This Court wishes to recall the principle of the judgment in *Niranjan Hemchandra Sashittal v. State of Maharashtra*. It held:

“‘When weighing corruption and its effect, it cannot be measured by the status of the person who committed the offence. Corruption destroys every component of a nation's advancement, including its growth and long-term vision, and ruins the nation. It disrupts economic development and buries it.’

“This case must be approached with that principle in mind. It is an excellent example of the abuse of power by those in authority and the greed of persons in positions of responsibility who seek wealth through wrongful means. Such conduct by those in power destroys the structure of a democratic country.”
Having underlined this earlier Supreme Court ruling, Judge Cunha went on to say more in his judgment. I shall tell you tomorrow what he said.
With affection,
M.K.
21 October 2013
